input_path: data\papers\Heithoff et al. - A Digital Shadow for Accurate Robot Motion Control Integrating Data with Friction Models.pdf

extracted_characteristics.system_under_study: Industrial serial robot MABI MAX 100, employed in manufacturing tasks such as machining and pick‑and‑place. The robot consists of a single-rotated axis driven by a permanent‑magnet synchronous motor coupled to a cycloidal gear reduction unit. It provides a functional reach of ≈ 2 m and an operating speed of up to 75 °/s, making it suitable for high‑precision, high‑repeatability operations. Its compact footprint and torque‑rich actuation enable integration into tight production cells while maintaining safety and reliability.
extracted_characteristics.dt_services:
The Digital Twin delivers real‑time monitoring of electrical and thermal state variables (motor current, joint temperature) and performs a continuous, multi‑physics‑aware identification of joint‑friction parameters using an online finite‑element model. These data are visualised on an interactive dashboard that shows current robot state, identified forces and friction trends, allowing operators to observe and analyse performance in situ. The services are targeted at process engineers and maintenance crews for diagnostics, preventive analysis and optimisation of the physical robot.
The Twin does not issue control commands back to the robot—it serves as a passive observer that collects data and provides analytical insights.
extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers: The Twin is built upon a SysML component‑and‑connector model that defines the robot’s functional and logical architecture. Executable simulation models are created in MathWorks MATLAB Simulink, enabling dynamic validation of the physical‑plant behaviour. Data from the physical robot are streamed into the simulation via a one‑way data flow, in line with the digital shadow reference model. The modeling and simulation workflow is supported by standard MBSE tools and a shared repository that stores model artifacts, simulation results and identified parameters.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data: Digital twin models are constructed using a hierarchical MBSE architecture comprising requirements, functional, logical, and product/technical layers. System structure is defined with architecture description languages (ADLs) such as ACME, AADL, MontiArc, and SysML IBDs. Behavioral aspects are captured with MontiArc Automaton or SysML v1.7 state machines, and are further refined using simulation tools such as MathWorks Simulink or executable code generated in C++/Java. Runtime data are supplied by a digital shadow that ingests one‑way data streams from the physical counterpart, aggregates and abstracts contextual trace data for analysis and control within the twin.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction: Physical-to-virtual data flow consists of time‑series sensor data streamed from the physical system via MQTT; the data originates from predefined sensor channels. The document specifies the use of MQTT but does not detail payload structures, exact sampling rates, or additional preprocessing steps.
extracted_characteristics.virtual_to_physical_interaction: The digital twin operates as a passive digital shadow and does not dispatch any control commands or actuation signals back to the physical system. Consequently, there are no virtual‑to‑physical command flows, validation, authentication mechanisms, or feedback loops to the physical asset. The twin is read‑only from the perspective of the physical machine.
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection: The connection uses MQTT as the messaging protocol. No further details on network topology, bandwidth, latency, reliability, or security are provided in the document.
extracted_characteristics.life_cycle_stages: The Digital Twin life‑cycle follows the MBSE practice of partitioning complexity into four abstraction layers: requirements, functional architecture, logical architecture, and product/technical layer. Each layer is represented by artefacts in modeling languages such as SysML (inter‑component diagrams), MontiArc, ACME or AADL. Transition management between levels is governed by architecture description languages (ADLs) that capture components and connectors, and by modelling methods such as RFLP (Requirements, Functions, Logics, Physics), OOSEM, SPES and SMArDT that provide systematic progression from high‑level requirements to deployable product models. Governance of the life‑cycle is achieved through traceability links and version control of model artefacts across abstraction levels, enabling controlled evolution from requirements to implementation and integration.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution: The twinning effort follows a four‑step engineering workflow: (1) Problem Identification – Define the fidelity level and performance goals for the DT; (2) Asset and Model Analysis – Examine existing physical asset data and any available analytical or computational models; (3) Use or Creation of Calculations or Models – Develop or adapt analytical formulas, simulation models (e.g., Simulink bodies, MontiArc automata) or code numerical solvers that capture the domain physics; and (4) Data Identification – Select the relevant sensor streams, historical data sets, and metadata that will be streamed into the DT. Methodologies such as RFLP, OOSEM, SPES, or SMArDT structure the activity, while quality assurance activities are performed at the model‑validation level (e.g., simulation benchmarking against measured data). Evolution of the twin is handled by incremental refinement of the MsBSE artifacts and by updating the data ingestion pipeline as new sensor modalities become available.
extracted_characteristics.insights_and_decision_making: The Digital Twin makes operational insights by calculating joint friction values within a gearbox subsystem and presenting these metrics on an interactive dashboard. The dashboard offers visualised time‑series of friction heat maps, allowing operators to spot anomalies or degradation. Current decision support is limited to threshold‑based alerts triggered when friction exceeds a predefined bound, and no advanced analytics or confidence metrics are described in the source material.
extracted_characteristics.standardization: The system adheres to widely adopted digital‑product frameworks: it implements Asset Administration Shell (AAS) containers to expose asset metadata, and it uses SysML for functional modelling and ACME/AADL for logical architecture. Web‑based data exchange occurs over MQTT, ensuring a lightweight publish/subscribe channel that supports industrial IoT interoperability. No specific ISO, IEC or IEEE standards were cited within the documented excerpt, beyond the general reference to SysML and MQTT as the chosen modelling language and communication protocol, respectively.

II. B ACKGROUND

To better understand our approach, we describe relevant
modeling methods, important concepts for DSs, and methods
on how to derive concrete DSs.


_A. Modeling Software and Systems_

To describe the structure and behavior of systems, ModelBased Systems-Engineering (MBSE) uses systems engineering
modeling languages such as SysML [17] during the development phase. Due to the complexity of systems, modeling
is typically split into different levels of abstraction, e.g.,
requirements, functional architecture, logical architecture, and
product/technical layer. There exist different methodologies
on how to handle this complexity, e.g., RFLP (Requirements, Functions, Logics, Physics) [18], [19], OOSEM [20],
SPES [21], or SMArDT [22]. To describe the functional
and logical architecture, typically architecture description languages (ADLs) are used. As main concepts, they describe
systems from a component & connector perspective, e.g.,
ACME [23], AADL [24], MontiArc [25], [26] or SysML
IBDs [17], [27]. These typically describe the system’s components, their interfaces, their interconnections, and composition.
Some ADLs also include methods to describe the system’s
behavior, e.g., MontiArc Automaton or state machines in
SysML v1.7. To describe behavior in more detail, these models
are often combined with further (modeling) tools such as
MathWorks Simulink [28], other simulation tools and methods [29], [30], or code in general-purpose languages like C++
or Java.

Thus, a comprehensive description of a system from an
MBSE perspective might have to cope with models in different
modeling notations and different tools. In addition, various
heterogeneous stakeholders and perspectives need to be integrated [31].


_B. Digital Shadow Reference Model_

Kritzinger et al. [32] differentiate digital shadows from
digital twins based on their communication flow. A digital
shadow is characterized by “ _an automated one-way data_
_flow between the state of an existing physical object and_
_a digital object_ [32]”. In practical terms, this means that a
digital shadow functions as a passive entity: it neither operates
autonomously nor pushes data back to the physical system.
Therefore, software systems require active components to
interpret and process this incoming data [33], [34]. A Digital
Twin (DT) can provide such active components [35]–[37].
In the remainder of this paper, we understand a digital
shadow as _“a set of contextual data traces and their ag-_
_gregation and abstraction collected for a specific purpose_



**Fig. 1:** Digital shadow reference model as a UML class
diagram. It defines important concepts for digital shadows in
manufacturing.

block_1_judge.reasoning: The extraction accurately describes the MABI MAX 100 robot, its components, and specifications. The source text confirms it is a 'serial robot MABI MAX 100' with 'six axes', used for 'machining'. The specific drive mechanism is detailed as 'a permanent magnet synchronous motor... followed by a cycloidal gear'. The operational specs such as 'functional reach of 2 m' and speed of '75 [°]/s' are directly supported. The text also mentions 'high path repeatability (0.06 mm)' and 'positional accuracy (0.1 mm)', supporting the precision claims.

The extraction correctly identifies the services as real-time monitoring and continuous friction identification. The abstract states the system enables 'continuous, multi-physics-aware identification of joint friction' using 'white-box and black-box models'. Section IV confirms the display of data in a 'dashboard'. The passive nature (one-way data flow) is supported by the digital shadow definition: 'an automated one-way data flow between the state of an existing physical object and a digital object'.

The extraction lists the tools accurately: SysML for modeling ('SysML component-and-connector model'), MathWorks Simulink for simulation ('executable simulation models of MathWorks MATLAB Simulink'), and MQTT for data streaming ('streamed via MQTT'). The alignment with the 'digital shadow reference model' is confirmed in the background section. However, the mention of a 'shared repository' is not explicitly found in the text, though the 'SystemModel' in Fig. 5 implies model storage. The rest is well-supported.

block_5_judge.reasoning: The extraction accurately details the MBSE abstraction layers and specific modeling languages/methods found in the Background section. It correctly lists the four abstraction layers ('requirements, functional architecture, logical architecture, and product/technical layer'), the specific modeling methods ('RFLP ..., OOSEM, SPES or SMArDT'), and the languages/ADLs ('ACME, AADL, MontiArc or SysML IBDs'). It is highly specific and well-grounded in the source.

The extraction perfectly captures the four-step workflow (Problem Identification, Asset and Model Analysis, Use or Creation of Calculations or Models, Data Identification) detailed in Sections II.C and IV.A. It correctly identifies specific tools like Simulink and MontiArc. The score is reduced because the 'Evolution' section speculates on 'updating the data ingestion pipeline as new sensor modalities become available,' which is not explicitly found in the provided text, though the text does mention 'continuously improving... during operation.'

While the extraction correctly identifies that the system calculates joint friction and uses a dashboard (supported by 'visualization in a dashboard' and 'detection of friction loss'), it includes specific unsupported details such as 'friction heat maps' and 'threshold-based alerts,' which are not present in the provided source text.

The extraction correctly identifies the use of Asset Administration Shells (AAS), SysML, and MQTT for data exchange, all of which are explicitly mentioned in the Abstract and Section IV.A. It notes the use of ACME/AADL as ADLs for logical architecture, which is supported by the background in Section II.A. The claim about ISO/IEC/IEEE standards not being cited is a reasonable interpretation of the text's focus on specific languages over regulatory standards.
